metadata.description: Poshan by Asha — Nepali & South Asian Recipe Library
metadata.notes: All nutrition values are estimates per standard serving. location_tags: home=home-cooked, restaurant=dine-in, takeaway=delivery/takeout, street=street food. sodium_mg: estimated per serving. is_side_dish: true for low-calorie side items not suitable as standalone meals.

recipes.description: Flattened rice (chiura) served with fresh whole-milk yogurt and seasonal fruits. A classic Nepali breakfast that requires zero cooking.
recipes.notes: Use whole-milk dahi for better satiety. Can add a teaspoon of honey for sweetness.

recipes.description: Traditional Nepali ring-shaped fried rice bread served with spicy tomato chutney. A festive favourite that doubles as a treat-meal breakfast.
recipes.notes: Batch cook and freeze. Reheat in a dry pan. Treat meal — higher in oil than everyday breakfast.

recipes.name_en: Dhido with Ghee and Gundruk Achar
recipes.description: Millet or buckwheat porridge cooked to a thick dough, served with ghee and fermented leafy green (gundruk) chutney. One of the most nutritious traditional Nepali breakfasts.
key_ingredients: kodo ko pitho (millet flour) or phaapar (buckwheat)
recipes.notes: Millet dhido has a lower glycaemic index than rice-based dishes. Excellent for clients with diabetes or blood sugar concerns.

recipes.name_en: Boiled Eggs with Whole Wheat Roti
recipes.description: Simple, protein-rich breakfast. Two boiled eggs with two whole wheat rotis. Can be eaten with a thin smear of ghee or achar.
recipes.notes: Great for clients needing a high-protein breakfast without cooking complexity. Prep rotis night before.

recipes.description: Rolled oats cooked in milk, topped with mixed nuts and a drizzle of honey. Quick, filling, and excellent for blood sugar management.
recipes.notes: Use whole milk for better satiety. Can add cinnamon — beneficial for blood sugar. Easily available in Kathmandu supermarkets.

recipes.description: Beaten rice tempered with mustard seeds, turmeric, green chilli, onion and curry leaves. A light and satisfying savoury breakfast.
recipes.notes: Very quick to make. Add a handful of roasted bhatmas on the side to boost protein.

recipes.description: Thin millet porridge — a traditional Nepali recovery and everyday breakfast. Gentle on digestion, nutritious, and deeply grounding.
recipes.notes: Finger millet is one of the highest calcium-containing grains. Excellent for women's bone health. Very low GI.

recipes.description: Whole wheat flatbread stuffed with spiced scrambled egg, cooked on a tawa with minimal oil. Filling, protein-rich, and genuinely delicious.
recipes.notes: Use minimal oil on tawa. Pairs well with dahi on the side.

recipes.description: Leftover cooked rice mixed with fresh dahi and a pinch of salt. A classic, practical Nepali breakfast using yesterday's rice — no waste, no fuss.
recipes.notes: Cooling and probiotic-rich. Excellent for gut health. Best in warmer months.

recipes.name_en: Bread with Peanut Butter and Banana
recipes.serving_description: 2 slices bread + 1.5 tbsp peanut butter + 1 banana
recipes.description: Whole grain bread with natural peanut butter and a banana. A quick, energy-dense breakfast for clients with active mornings or no time to cook.
recipes.notes: Use natural peanut butter only — check label for no added sugar or palm oil. Available at most Kathmandu supermarkets.

recipes.description: The cornerstone of Nepali nutrition. Cooked rice, lentil soup (masoor or musuro dal), and seasonal vegetable curry. Nutritionally complete, culturally irreplaceable.
recipes.notes: Batch cook dal and tarkari. Switch to brown rice for lower GI. Add a spoon of ghee for fat-soluble vitamin absorption. This is the foundation meal — never eliminate it.

recipes.description: The protein-forward version of dal bhat. Standard Nepali chicken curry (home-style with turmeric, garlic, ginger, onion) added to the classic plate.
recipes.notes: Batch cook the chicken curry. Excellent post-workout meal. Reduce rice portion slightly if targeting lower carbs.

recipes.description: Steamed dumplings filled with minced buffalo meat, onion, garlic, ginger and Nepali spices. A Kathmandu favourite — and steamed is significantly lower calorie than fried.
recipes.notes: Steamed not fried — important distinction for calories. Batch make and freeze uncooked. Treat meal that's secretly reasonable calorie-wise.

recipes.description: Steamed dumplings with a filling of cabbage, carrot, onion, garlic, ginger and tofu or paneer. Low calorie and genuinely satisfying.
recipes.notes: Adding paneer or tofu to the filling boosts protein significantly. Great treat meal that won't blow the day's targets.

recipes.description: Tibetan-origin noodle soup with shredded chicken, vegetables and a deeply savoury broth. Popular across Kathmandu — warming, filling, and relatively well-balanced.
recipes.notes: Can substitute rice noodles for gluten-free version. Batch cook broth and add noodles fresh.

recipes.description: Leftover rice stir-fried with mixed vegetables, soy sauce, garlic and a touch of sesame oil. A practical, quick Kathmandu lunch.
recipes.notes: Day-old rice is better than fresh — lower GI due to retrogradation of starch. Add an egg for extra protein (becomes +78 cal, +6g protein).

recipes.description: Day-old rice stir-fried with diced chicken breast, vegetables, soy sauce and egg. A popular quick-service and home lunch across Kathmandu.
recipes.notes: Use chicken breast to keep fat lower. Control oil — 1 tbsp max.

recipes.description: Traditional Newari sprouted mixed bean soup made from nine varieties of beans and legumes. One of the most nutritionally complete traditional dishes in Nepali cuisine.
key_ingredients: mixed sprouted beans (soybean, black-eyed pea, mung bean, chickpea, field pea, moth bean, rice bean, horse gram, fava bean)
recipes.notes: Sprouting increases bioavailability of nutrients significantly. Batch cook and freeze. High protein and fibre — excellent for PCOS and diabetes management.

recipes.description: Dry-style potato and cauliflower curry with cumin, turmeric and coriander. Served with three whole wheat rotis. A household staple across Nepal and North India.
recipes.notes: Batch cook the curry. Cauliflower is rich in glucosinolates — beneficial for thyroid health when cooked (reduces goitrogens).

recipes.description: Spinach or mixed leafy green curry with paneer cubes. Rich in iron, calcium and protein — particularly valuable for female clients.
recipes.notes: Iron from spinach is better absorbed when paired with vitamin C — add a squeeze of lime. Batch cook base sauce and add paneer fresh.

recipes.description: Stir-fried wheat noodles with cabbage, carrot, onion, capsicum and soy sauce. A ubiquitous Nepali street and home meal.
recipes.notes: Add an egg or tofu for extra protein. Control oil carefully — easy to overdo.

recipes.description: Stir-fried wheat noodles with shredded chicken, vegetables and soy-based sauce. A popular Kathmandu lunch that hits protein targets well.
recipes.notes: Use chicken breast. Add extra vegetables to increase fibre without adding much calories.

recipes.description: Fermented leafy green (gundruk) simmered in a light soup with tomato, onion and spices, served alongside rice. A probiotic-rich, fibre-dense traditional lunch.
recipes.notes: Gundruk is a traditional Nepali fermented food with probiotic benefits. Low in calories, high in flavour. Excellent light lunch for weight loss clients.

recipes.description: Spiced chickpea curry with onion, tomato, garlic and whole spices. Served with whole wheat roti. One of the most fibre-and-protein-rich plant-based lunches.
recipes.notes: Batch cook and refrigerate for 4–5 days. High fibre makes this excellent for PCOS and blood sugar management.

recipes.description: Hard-boiled or pan-fried eggs simmered in a tomato-onion-spice gravy, served with rice. An affordable, protein-rich Nepali meal.
recipes.notes: Very economical and protein-rich. Batch cook the curry base and add eggs fresh. Ideal for clients on a budget.

recipes.description: Spiced button or oyster mushroom curry with tomato and onion base, served with rice. Mushrooms are one of the few plant sources of Vitamin D.
recipes.notes: Good source of plant-based protein. Oyster mushrooms have higher protein content than button mushrooms.

recipes.name_en: Buff Sukuti with Rice and Achar
recipes.description: Traditional Nepali dried buffalo meat, pan-fried or sautéed with spices and served with rice and achar. A high-protein, iron-rich traditional option.
recipes.notes: High in protein and iron. Good for clients with iron deficiency anaemia. Sukuti is available pre-made at Nepali stores.

recipes.description: Red lentil dal served over brown rice — a nutritional upgrade on the classic. Brown rice significantly lowers the glycaemic impact compared to white rice.
recipes.notes: Brown rice requires longer soaking (2 hours). The extra fibre is worth it for clients managing blood sugar or PCOS.

recipes.description: Newari rice flour crepe topped with spiced minced meat or egg and seasonal vegetables. Often called the 'Nepali pizza' — thin, crispy and satisfying.
recipes.notes: Naturally gluten-free. Treat meal status due to richness of topping. Great for introducing Newari cuisine to clients.

recipes.name_en: Paalungo Dal (Spinach Lentil Soup) with Roti
recipes.description: Lentil soup enriched with fresh spinach and tempered with mustard seeds, garlic and dried chilli. Iron-rich and deeply nourishing.
recipes.notes: Iron absorption enhanced by the vitamin C in the roti. Excellent for female clients with iron deficiency or PCOS.

recipes.description: A lighter evening version of the classic dal bhat — reduced rice portion, more dal, and a fresh cucumber-tomato salad on the side. Digestion-friendly for dinner.
recipes.notes: Reducing carbohydrates at dinner is a practical strategy for fat loss without removing the meal clients love. Use the batch-cooked dal from lunch.

recipes.name_en: Grilled Chicken with Roti and Salad
recipes.description: Marinated grilled chicken breast (with Nepali spices — cumin, coriander, turmeric, garlic, ginger) served with two rotis and a fresh salad.
recipes.notes: Highest protein dinner in the library. Ideal for gym-going clients or those on aggressive fat loss. Marinate overnight for best flavour.

recipes.description: Home-style Nepali chicken curry with whole spices, onion, garlic, ginger and tomato — served with whole wheat roti.
recipes.notes: Batch cook curry. Bone-in chicken adds more flavour and collagen. Watch oil quantity during tempering.

recipes.description: Nepali-style fish curry with mustard oil, turmeric, tomato and chilli. Rohu, catla or tilapia are common in Kathmandu markets.
recipes.notes: Rich in omega-3 fatty acids. Excellent for inflammation and brain health. Encourage clients to eat fish at least twice a week.

recipes.description: Fragrant basmati rice cooked with whole spices, mixed vegetables and a touch of ghee. A simple but special dinner.
recipes.notes: Batch cook and refrigerate. Add a side of dahi to complete protein profile. Basmati has a lower GI than standard white rice.

recipes.description: Spiced scrambled eggs with onion, tomato, green chilli and coriander served with whole wheat rotis. A quick, protein-rich weeknight dinner.
recipes.notes: Very quick dinner — 15 minutes. Reduce oil to 1 tsp for lower calorie version. Excellent for busy evenings.

recipes.name_en: Jhol Momo (Steamed Momo in Spicy Broth)
recipes.description: Steamed buff dumplings served in a rich, spicy tomato-sesame-chilli broth (jhol). Kathmandu street food at its finest — warming, deeply flavoured, and genuinely satisfying.
recipes.notes: Jhol Momo is steamed (not fried) so significantly lower calorie than R013 without the broth. The jhol is what makes this special — timur pepper is essential. Batch make filling and freeze.

recipes.description: Traditional Newari curry of potato, fermented bamboo shoot (tama) and black-eyed beans. A distinctive, probiotic-rich dish with complex layered flavours.
recipes.notes: Tama is a traditional Nepali fermented ingredient with probiotic properties. High fibre from beans. Rich cultural dish worth introducing to clients.

recipes.description: Fragrant layered rice and chicken dish with whole spices, fried onion and saffron. A celebratory meal that feels like a treat but provides solid protein.
recipes.notes: Treat meal for special occasions or weekly reward. Batch cook and refrigerate well. Serve with raita to balance.

recipes.description: Black lentil dal slow-cooked with garlic, ginger and a mustard oil tempering. One of the most nutrient-dense dal varieties available in Nepal.
recipes.notes: Requires longer cooking time but worth it — black lentils have higher iron and protein than red lentils. Excellent for female clients.

recipes.name_en: Tofu and Vegetable Stir-fry with Rice
recipes.description: Firm tofu pan-fried with mixed vegetables, garlic, soy sauce and sesame oil over rice. A plant-based protein option that satisfies.
recipes.notes: Press tofu before cooking to remove moisture and improve texture. Tofu is a complete plant protein. Good for vegetarian clients needing protein boost.

recipes.description: Light Nepali-style chicken broth with vegetables and turmeric. Low calorie, high protein. Ideal for recovery days or lighter dinner targets.
recipes.notes: Lowest calorie dinner option in the library. Excellent for clients needing to keep dinner calories low. Bone-in chicken gives rich broth.

recipes.description: Paneer cubes in a spiced tomato-onion gravy with capsicum and peas. Rich in calcium and protein — a satisfying vegetarian dinner.
recipes.notes: Homemade paneer is lower fat than commercial. Use low-fat milk if available. High calcium — important for female clients.

recipes.description: Buckwheat flatbread served with lentil dal. Buckwheat is naturally gluten-free with a lower GI than wheat — an excellent swap for clients managing blood sugar.
recipes.notes: Buckwheat is underutilised in urban Kathmandu but widely available. Push this for clients with diabetes or insulin resistance.

recipes.description: Newari savoury pancakes made from ground black lentil batter, pan-fried until golden. Can be topped with egg (wo) for extra protein. A culturally significant and delicious dinner.
recipes.notes: Naturally gluten-free. Add an egg on top (wo) for +6g protein. A wonderful way to introduce Newari cuisine into the meal plan.

recipes.description: Dry-roasted whole soybeans — a traditional Nepali street snack that is extremely high in plant protein. Often sold at stalls across Kathmandu.
recipes.notes: One of the highest protein snacks in Nepali food culture. Readily available and affordable. Keep a bag at work or in the bag.

recipes.description: Seasonal fruits — mango, papaya, banana, guava or apple — with a bowl of fresh whole-milk dahi. A naturally sweet, probiotic-rich snack.
recipes.notes: Use low-sugar seasonal fruits. Pair with protein-rich dahi to slow sugar absorption. An excellent sweet craving fix.

recipes.description: Dry-roasted black chickpeas — a crunchy, satisfying snack widely available across Nepal. High in fibre and protein, low on fat.
recipes.notes: Buy pre-roasted from any Nepali store or taat pasal. Better for blood sugar than processed snacks. Eat with a squeeze of lime and chilli for extra flavour.

recipes.description: Fresh whole-milk dahi with a small drizzle of honey. Probiotic-rich, protein-providing, and sweet enough to satisfy a dessert craving without the calories.
recipes.notes: Lowest calorie satisfying snack in the library. Excellent for clients with late-night sweet cravings.

recipes.description: Roasted lotus seeds — light, crunchy, very low calorie. Roast in a dry pan with a pinch of salt or chilli powder.
recipes.notes: Available at supermarkets and dry goods stores in Kathmandu. Very low calorie for volume — good for clients who snack out of boredom.

recipes.description: Boiled corn on the cob — a classic Nepali street snack. Squeeze of lime and a pinch of salt takes it to the next level.
recipes.notes: Classic afternoon snack widely available across Kathmandu. Satisfying, filling and naturally low in fat.

recipes.description: Two hard-boiled eggs with a pinch of salt and chilli powder. The most portable, affordable high-protein snack available.
recipes.notes: Batch boil 6 eggs and refrigerate for the week. Extremely convenient protein source. Best snack for clients with physically demanding jobs.

recipes.description: Roasted peanuts tossed with lime juice, salt, chilli powder and chopped coriander. A satisfying, protein-and-fat snack that keeps hunger at bay.
recipes.notes: Portion control is key — 40g is the right serving. Easily over-eaten. Buy raw peanuts and dry-roast at home for healthiest version.

recipes.description: Rice slow-cooked in whole milk with sugar, cardamom and sometimes saffron. A traditional Nepali celebratory dessert. Use smaller portion for fat-loss days.
recipes.notes: Treat dessert. Can reduce sugar by 30% without affecting taste much. Batch cook and serve in small bowls to manage portions.

recipes.description: Strained yogurt sweetened with sugar or honey, flavoured with cardamom, saffron and mixed dried fruits. A Newari speciality — the healthiest dessert in this library.
recipes.notes: Lower calorie than most desserts. Protein-forward compared to sweets. Excellent treat-meal dessert that fits into a fat-loss plan comfortably.

recipes.description: Traditional Nepali sour-sweet candy made from lapsi (Nepali hog plum). A beloved Nepali treat that satisfies sweet-and-sour cravings at very low calorie cost.
recipes.notes: Best low-calorie sweet option in the library. Widely available everywhere in Nepal. Perfect for clients with sweet cravings post-dinner.

recipes.description: The famous Bhaktapur sweetened yogurt, set in clay pots. Creamy, lightly sweet, probiotic-rich. A Nepali cultural treasure and a genuinely healthy dessert option.
recipes.notes: Available freshly made in Bhaktapur and most Kathmandu stores. Probiotic and calcium-rich. Frame this as a treat that is actually good for gut health.

recipes.description: Mixed seasonal fruits — papaya, mango, guava, apple, banana — tossed with lime juice, a pinch of rock salt and chilli powder. The lightest dessert in the library.
recipes.notes: Lowest calorie dessert option. Excellent for clients who want something sweet post-dinner without breaking the calorie budget. Use lower-sugar fruits for diabetics.

recipes.description: Wild fiddlehead ferns flash-stir-fried with garlic, turmeric and mustard oil. A seasonal Nepali delicacy rich in vitamins A and C. Available in spring across Nepal.
recipes.notes: Seasonal — available spring/early summer in Nepal. Blanch briefly before stir-frying to remove bitterness. One of the highest vitamin A sources in traditional Nepali cooking.

recipes.description: Tangy soup made from sinki — fermented radish taproots. A traditional Nepali probiotic food served as a light soup or pickle accompaniment. Deeply supportive of gut health.
recipes.notes: Sinki is produced by fermenting mula (radish) taproots in pits — a traditional preservation technique. Strong probiotic properties. Pair with rice for a complete light meal.

recipes.description: Bottle gourd (lauki/ghiya) cooked in a light tomato-based curry with cumin and coriander. Extremely hydrating, easy to digest, and one of the lowest-calorie curries you can make.
recipes.notes: Lauki is 96% water — making this one of the most hydrating cooked meals available. Traditional weight management food. A surprisingly satisfying curry despite its low calorie count.

recipes.description: Sautéed okra with double onions, dry-roasted spices, and a minimal amount of oil. Okra is packed with soluble fibre and folate — excellent for blood sugar control and women's health.
recipes.notes: The mucilage in okra is excellent for slowing sugar absorption. Dry okra before cooking and use a non-stick pan to keep oil minimal. Critical not to cover while cooking — keeps it from going slimy.

recipes.description: Eggplant roasted directly over an open flame until charred, then mashed with onion, tomato, garlic and spices. The smoking gives incredible depth of flavour with minimal fat. Rich in anthocyanins.
recipes.notes: The flame-roasting is essential — it creates the smoky flavour without any added fat. Eggplant skin contains nasunin, a powerful brain-protective antioxidant. Batch-roast multiple aubergines at once.

recipes.description: South Indian-style dry stir-fry with asparagus and carrot, tempered with mustard seeds, curry leaves, dried red chilli and a small amount of fresh coconut. Vibrant, quick and full of micronutrients.
recipes.notes: Poriyal (dry stir-fry) is one of the healthiest South Asian cooking techniques — flash-cook preserves vitamins. Asparagus available in Kathmandu supermarkets. Excellent micronutrient side dish.

recipes.description: Finely chopped cucumber, tomato and onion with fresh lemon juice, black salt and coriander. Adds significant volume and micronutrients to any meal for almost zero calories.
recipes.notes: Lowest calorie item in the entire library. Serve alongside any lunch or dinner to add bulk without adding calories. The black salt adds a distinctive sulphurous depth that elevates this beyond a plain salad.

recipes.description: Napa cabbage quickly sautéed with rehydrated shiitake mushrooms, garlic, ginger and a light soy-sesame dressing. Napa cabbage is high in Vitamin C; shiitake contains lentinan which supports immunity.
recipes.notes: Dried shiitake (available in Kathmandu Asian grocery stores) intensify flavour when rehydrated. Soaking water is rich in nutrients — use it as part of the sauce. Very low calorie side dish.

recipes.description: Chinese-style cucumber appetiser — cucumbers smashed (not sliced) and dressed with garlic, rice vinegar, sesame oil and chilli flakes. Smashing creates crevices that absorb the dressing. Anti-inflammatory and almost calorie-free.
recipes.notes: The smashing technique is key — it changes the texture and flavour absorption dramatically. Rice vinegar and sesame oil available at most Kathmandu supermarkets. Excellent palate cleanser or pre-meal appetite regulator.

recipes.description: Baby bok choy steamed until tender, finished with a hot garlic oil drizzle and light soy sauce. Bok choy provides exceptional levels of vitamins A, C and K, calcium and iron in very few calories.
recipes.notes: One of the most nutrient-dense vegetables per calorie in the library. Particularly valuable for female clients — the calcium and iron profile is excellent. Available in Kathmandu supermarkets year-round.

recipes.description: Sliced lotus root stir-fried with garlic, ginger, a touch of soy sauce and sesame oil. High in dietary fibre and Vitamin C. The distinctive crunchy texture makes it deeply satisfying.
recipes.notes: Lotus root is available at some Kathmandu specialty markets and Newari food suppliers. Soak sliced lotus root in vinegar water before cooking to prevent browning. The crunch is what makes it satisfying.

recipes.description: Japanese mizuna or tatsoi greens quickly sautéed with garlic and a splash of soy sauce. Milder than spinach, concentrated in micronutrients, and ready in under 10 minutes.
recipes.notes: Can substitute spinach or any tender leafy green if unavailable. Tatsoi has a mild, slightly mustard flavour — excellent for clients who find spinach bitter. Available in upmarket Kathmandu supermarkets.

recipes.description: Grated cauliflower stir-fried with turmeric, cumin and a pinch of salt to mimic rice. Reduces carbohydrate intake by 75% compared to white rice while adding Vitamin C and fibre.
recipes.notes: Smart swap — use alongside (or instead of) regular rice for clients managing blood sugar or on aggressive fat loss. Grate using the large side of a box grater or pulse in a food processor. Do not overcook — keep slightly firm.

recipes.description: Flatbread made with a blend of oat flour or coconut flour mixed with whole wheat flour. Significantly higher in fibre and lower on the glycaemic index than standard white flour roti.
recipes.notes: Smart swap for standard roti. The 50/50 blend keeps the texture manageable — 100% oat/coconut flour is too crumbly. Recommend for clients managing blood sugar or PCOS. Coconut flour adds healthy fats.

recipes.description: Low-fat yogurt whisked smooth and mixed with grated cucumber or pomegranate seeds, a pinch of cumin and fresh mint. A probiotic-rich side that doubles as a light snack between meals.
recipes.notes: Smart swap for heavy dips or sweet snacks. Pomegranate version is higher in antioxidants. Can be served alongside any main meal to add protein and probiotics without significant calories.

recipes.description: Two slices of thin-crust pizza loaded with mixed vegetables and mozzarella. Ordering pizza? Thin crust, vegetable toppings, and stopping at 2 slices keeps this manageable within a fat-loss plan.
recipes.notes: Smart restaurant order — choose thin crust over stuffed/thick, go heavy on veg toppings, avoid extra cheese. 2 slices is a satisfying portion that fits the day's targets. Treat meal, not off-limits.

recipes.description: Chinese-style stir-fried noodles with cabbage, carrot, capsicum, spring onion, garlic and soy sauce. A lighter version of the Kathmandu restaurant staple — made at home with controlled oil.
recipes.notes: Home version uses 1 tbsp oil vs restaurants often using 3-4 tbsp. Add tofu or egg for protein boost. Restaurant version will be higher calorie — treat as an estimate when dining out.

recipes.description: Day-old rice wok-fried with egg, spring onion, soy sauce, garlic and a touch of sesame oil. The Chinese-restaurant version of fried rice — cleaner and lighter than the Nepali equivalent.
recipes.notes: Day-old refrigerated rice is essential — fresh rice turns mushy. Restaurant portions often 350-400g; ask for less rice or share. Home version easily controlled.

recipes.description: Italian pasta tossed with garlic, olive oil, dried chilli flakes and parsley. One of the simplest, fastest pasta dishes — and one of the lowest calorie options if you control the oil.
recipes.notes: Available in Kathmandu supermarkets. Control oil to max 1.5 tbsp per serving. Add a side of Kachumber salad (R065) to boost fibre and volume. Surprisingly filling for the calorie count.

recipes.description: Thai stir-fried rice noodles with tofu, egg, bean sprouts, spring onion and a tangy tamarind-soy sauce, finished with crushed peanuts and lime. A satisfying balanced plate from a single pan.
recipes.notes: Rice noodles and tamarind available in Asian grocery stores in Kathmandu. Substitute fish sauce with soy for vegetarian version. Peanut topping adds healthy fats — do not skip.

recipes.description: Delicate chicken or prawn wontons simmered in a clear ginger-garlic broth with bok choy and spring onion. One of the lowest-calorie satisfying dinners available when dining out.
recipes.notes: Excellent low-calorie restaurant choice. When ordering out, ask for steamed rather than fried wontons. Home version: batch make and freeze uncooked wontons for quick weeknight dinners.

recipes.description: Steamed Chinese-style dumplings filled with cabbage, carrot, shiitake mushroom, garlic and ginger. The healthy sibling of the momo family — light, satisfying, and easy to batch freeze.
recipes.notes: Batch make and freeze uncooked — steam directly from frozen (add 3 extra minutes). Excellent low-calorie snack or light dinner starter. Dipping sauce: soy + rice vinegar + chilli.

recipes.description: Penne or fusilli tossed in a simple blended tomato sauce with garlic, basil and a drizzle of olive oil. A practical, affordable weeknight dinner that clients new to cooking can manage easily.
recipes.notes: Canned tomatoes and pasta available at most Kathmandu supermarkets. Add spinach or chickpeas to boost nutrition. Batch cook sauce and refrigerate for 5 days. Lowest fat pasta option in the library.

recipes.name_en: Soaked Chana Salad with Fresh Fruits
recipes.description: Overnight-soaked chickpeas served cold as a breakfast salad alongside a bowl of seasonal mixed fruits. High satiety, zero cooking required.
recipes.notes: Soak chana overnight for 8+ hours. Add a pinch of chaat masala and lemon juice for flavour. Pairs well with black coffee.

recipes.name_en: Brown Bread Egg and Chicken Sandwich
recipes.serving_description: 1 sandwich (1 brown bread slice, 2 eggs, 50g chicken, 50g veggies)
recipes.description: A protein-packed open sandwich on brown bread layered with egg omelette, grilled chicken strips, fresh veggies, and optional half-avocado for healthy fats.
recipes.notes: Use spray oil or 2g oil to cook egg and chicken. Avocado adds healthy fat — include if within calorie target.

recipes.serving_description: 1 bowl moong salad (80g sprouts) + 1 portion papaya (~100g)
recipes.description: Sprouted or soaked green moong tossed with cucumber, onion, coriander, lemon, and chaat masala — served alongside fresh papaya. Light, enzyme-rich, and high in plant protein.
recipes.notes: Soak moong overnight or sprout for 12–24 hrs for better digestibility. Skip papaya if managing blood sugar closely.

recipes.serving_description: 1 bowl boiled chana + 1 bowl mixed fruits
recipes.description: Pressure-cooked whole chickpeas (batch prep) served warm or cold with a side of seasonal mixed fruits. Simple, filling, and macro-friendly.
recipes.notes: Batch cook chana and refrigerate for up to 4 days. Season with rock salt and lemon if desired.

recipes.description: Savoury oats pancake (chilla) made with rolled oats, veggies, green chilli, and spices — cooked on a non-stick pan with minimal oil, served with low-fat curd.
recipes.notes: Blend oats to a coarse flour or use as is for texture. Use 2–3g oil or spray on a non-stick pan.

recipes.name_en: Brown Bread Egg Omelette with Veggies
recipes.serving_description: 2 brown bread slices + 1 whole egg omelette with veggies
recipes.description: A quick breakfast of 2 toasted brown bread slices served alongside a fluffy omelette loaded with onion, capsicum, tomato, and seasonal vegetables.
recipes.notes: Use a non-stick pan with 2g oil or cooking spray. Load the omelette generously with veggies to increase fibre and volume.

recipes.description: A filling breakfast smoothie blending low-fat milk, banana, rolled oats, mixed nuts, and stevia — no added sugar, quick to prepare.
recipes.notes: Use stevia or skip sweetener entirely if banana is ripe. Can substitute milk with plant-based milk for a dairy-free version.

recipes.description: Creamy smoothie made with ripe avocado, low-fat milk, and mixed nuts. Rich in healthy monounsaturated fats and filling enough to carry you through the morning.
recipes.notes: Best consumed immediately after blending. Adjust thickness with milk. Pairs well with chia seeds soaked overnight.

recipes.description: Antioxidant-rich fruit smoothie blending strawberries, banana, blueberries, and almond milk. Bright, naturally sweet, and loaded with polyphenols.
recipes.notes: Use unsweetened almond milk. Frozen berries work well. Add a tablespoon of chia or flax seeds for extra fibre and omega-3.

recipes.name_ne: भिजेको बदाम अखरोट र अण्डा थाली
recipes.serving_description: 5 almonds + 2 walnuts + 1 whole egg + 1 egg white + 1 bread slice or fruit or avocado
recipes.description: A balanced breakfast platter: overnight-soaked peeled almonds and walnuts, a boiled whole egg and egg white, plus one serving of carbs — bread, a seasonal fruit, or half an avocado.
recipes.notes: Peel soaked almonds before eating — skin removal reduces phytic acid load. Rotate the carb side for variety.

recipes.name_ne: कम फ्याट पनीर स्टर फ्राई थाली
recipes.serving_description: Rice 120g cooked or 2 roti + paneer 100g + dal 100g + veggies 150g + salad 100g
recipes.description: Low-fat paneer cubes stir-fried with onion, capsicum, and spices (4g oil, non-stick pan), served with steamed rice or roti, a small portion of dal, and fresh salad.
recipes.notes: Use non-stick pan with 4g oil max. Low-fat paneer keeps calories reasonable while maintaining protein. Batch prep dal for the week.

recipes.serving_description: Rice 120g or 2 roti + soya chunks 30g raw + dal 120g + veggies 150g + salad 100g
recipes.description: Soya chunks (textured soy protein) rehydrated and stir-fried with veggies in minimal oil — served over rice or with roti, dal, and a fresh cucumber-carrot-beetroot salad.
recipes.notes: Soak soya chunks in hot water for 15 mins then squeeze dry before cooking. They double in volume when rehydrated.

recipes.serving_description: Rice 120g or 2 roti + gedagudi curry 120g + veggies 150g + salad 100g
recipes.description: Nepali-style black-eyed pea (gedagudi/lobia) curry slow-cooked with onion, tomato, ginger, and cumin — served with rice or roti and a fresh salad. A high-fibre, plant-protein lunch staple.
recipes.notes: Gedagudi needs 6–8 hrs soaking before cooking. Batch cook and refrigerate for 3 days.

recipes.serving_description: Rice 120g or 2 roti + rajma 100g cooked + 2 whole eggs + salad 70g
recipes.description: Cooked kidney beans (rajma) in a light onion-tomato masala served with two whole eggs (boiled or poached) over rice or with roti and a small green salad.
recipes.notes: Use 3g oil only. Eggs can be hard-boiled and added on the side, or cracked into the rajma gravy. High protein + fibre combo keeps hunger at bay.

recipes.name_en: Mung Dal Roti with Vegetable and Egg
recipes.name_ne: मुङ दाल रोटी तरकारी र अण्डा
recipes.serving_description: 2 mung dal roti + seasonal vegetable + 1 egg (any style)
recipes.description: Thin mung dal (yellow split moong) flatbreads cooked on a tawa, served with a stir-fried seasonal vegetable and one egg prepared to preference.
recipes.notes: Mung dal roti batter: soak split moong 2 hrs, grind with spices, cook thin on tawa. Higher protein than wheat roti.

recipes.description: Soft whole-wheat roti wraps filled with grilled spiced chicken strips, crunchy fresh veggies, and a light mint-coriander chutney. A clean, portable lunch option.
recipes.notes: Marinate chicken in yogurt and spices 30 mins ahead. Grill or cook with minimal oil on non-stick pan. Load with raw veggies for crunch and volume.

recipes.description: Cooked quinoa tossed with cucumber, tomato, carrot, onion, herbs, lemon, and olive oil — a complete plant protein meal with all essential amino acids.
recipes.notes: Rinse quinoa thoroughly before cooking to remove bitter saponins. Add chickpeas or boiled egg to boost protein further.

recipes.serving_description: 40g chiura (raw) + 1 whole egg omelette or soyabean with onion and spices
recipes.description: Raw chiura (beaten rice) paired with a spiced egg omelette — or a soyabean-onion stir fry for a plant-based version. Quick, satisfying, and higher protein than plain chiura.
recipes.notes: Raw chiura requires no cooking — just rehydrate lightly if preferred. Swap egg for soyabean granules for a vegan version.

recipes.serving_description: 100g sweet boiled corn + lime + salt + chilli
recipes.description: Sweet boiled corn kernels seasoned with rock salt, lime juice, and red chilli powder. A street-style snack made at home — crunchy, tangy, and naturally low-fat.
recipes.notes: Optionally add chaat masala or coriander. Avoid adding butter. Pairs with 1 glass of plain water or buttermilk.

recipes.serving_description: 100g boiled potato + ½ cup cucumber + ½ onion + masala
recipes.description: Home-style aloo chaat: boiled potato cubes tossed with diced cucumber, onion, lemon juice, chaat masala, and coriander — no frying, no oil.
recipes.notes: A much cleaner version than restaurant aloo chaat — no sev or fried additions. Boil potatoes ahead and refrigerate.

recipes.serving_description: 1 paratha (stuffed with ~40g paneer) cooked in 5g oil
recipes.description: Whole-wheat flatbread stuffed with spiced crumbled paneer, cooked on tawa with just 5g oil. A satisfying snack or light meal option.
recipes.notes: Use low-fat paneer to reduce calories further. Pair with curd or a small green chutney. Limit to 1 paratha as a snack.

recipes.serving_description: 50g boiled chana + 20g puffed rice + onion + tomato + coriander + spices
recipes.description: Bhel-style chaat combining boiled chickpeas and puffed rice with chopped onion, tomato, coriander, rock salt, chilli, and lemon juice. Crunchy, tangy, protein-rich, and zero oil.
recipes.notes: Toss just before eating so puffed rice stays crunchy. A great Nepali-street-style snack that works well as a 4–5 PM meal.

recipes.serving_description: 1 medium fruit + 5 pcs pistachio or 5 pcs cashew + lemon water with black salt
recipes.description: A structured afternoon snack: one medium seasonal fruit with a small portion of pistachios or cashews for healthy fat, plus lemon water with black salt to aid digestion.
recipes.notes: Avoid fruit juices — eat whole fruit for fibre. Walk 10 mins after this snack. Rotate between mango, papaya, guava, apple, and pear seasonally.

recipes.description: Classic Nepali dumplings stuffed with spiced cabbage, onion, ginger, and light oil — steamed, not fried. A lighter vegetarian momo option lower in calories than buff or chicken versions.
recipes.notes: Use minimal oil (5g) in the filling. Serve with tomato-chilli achar. Steam for 12–15 mins. Batch-freeze raw momos for quick dinners.

recipes.description: Low-fat paneer cubes marinated in spiced yogurt and grilled on a tawa or grill pan until slightly charred — served alongside a generous fresh salad. A clean, high-protein, low-carb dinner.
recipes.notes: Marinate paneer for at least 30 mins. Grill on high heat for char marks without extra oil. Good dinner option for days with a heavier carb lunch.

recipes.description: Whole-wheat roti wraps filled with stir-fried soyabean granules, capsicum, onion, and mixed veggies — a plant-protein dinner wrap that's filling and macro-balanced.
recipes.notes: Rehydrate soya granules in hot water 10 mins before stir-frying. Season well with cumin, chilli, and coriander for best flavour.

recipes.description: Crumbled paneer scrambled with onion, tomato, green chilli, and spices — a vegetarian spin on egg bhurji, served with two whole-wheat rotis and a side of steamed vegetables.
recipes.notes: Use low-fat paneer. Keep masala simple — just cumin, turmeric, and chilli. Don't overcook or paneer becomes rubbery.

recipes.name_en: Grilled Paneer and Mushroom Fried Rice
recipes.name_ne: ग्रिल्ड पनीर र च्याउ फ्राइड राइस
recipes.serving_description: 120g grilled paneer or mushroom + 160g fried rice (use more veggies)
recipes.description: Lightly grilled paneer or sautéed mushrooms served alongside a high-veggie rice stir-fry made with minimal oil. Using yesterday's cold rice works best for texture.
recipes.notes: Use cold pre-cooked rice for better fried rice texture. Load up on veggies — aim for 2:1 veggies-to-rice ratio. Choose mushroom for a lower calorie version.

recipes.serving_description: 8 piece wheat momo + 1 bowl chicken soup (no cream/butter)
recipes.description: Whole-wheat flour momo stuffed with spiced chicken mince, served alongside a clear chicken broth with vegetables — no butter or cream. A lighter Nepali dumpling dinner.
recipes.notes: Whole wheat dough gives extra fibre versus plain flour. Keep soup broth clear — season with salt, pepper, and herbs only.

recipes.serving_description: 100g quinoa cooked + 120g grilled chicken + salad items
recipes.description: Cooked quinoa tossed with grilled spiced chicken, cucumber, tomato, red onion, and a light lemon-herb dressing. A complete protein dinner that is light yet highly satisfying.
recipes.notes: Marinate chicken simply with cumin, salt, and lemon. Grill or pan-cook with minimal oil. Best assembled fresh before serving.

recipes.serving_description: 30g soyabean (raw) + 120g rice cooked into pulao + salad + raita
recipes.description: A one-pot soyabean rice pilaf: rehydrated soya granules cooked into spiced basmati rice with whole spices, onion, and aromatics — served with yogurt raita and salad.
recipes.notes: Soak soya granules 10 mins in hot water, squeeze out moisture before adding to rice. Reduces the beany taste significantly.

recipes.name_en: Wheat Noodles with Chicken and Veggies
recipes.description: Boiled whole-wheat noodles stir-fried with chunky veggies and grilled chicken strips — a lighter alternative to restaurant-style noodles with far less oil and no MSG.
recipes.notes: Boil noodles al dente and rinse cold to prevent sticking. Use whole-wheat noodles rather than refined for extra fibre. Load with veggies — capsicum, cabbage, carrot.

recipes.description: Sliced grilled chicken breast over a bed of mixed greens, cherry tomatoes, cucumber, red onion, and capsicum — dressed with olive oil, lemon juice, and a pinch of oregano. High protein, low carb, and genuinely satisfying. One of the most popular home-cooked continental lunches.
recipes.notes: Marinate chicken in garlic, lemon, olive oil, salt, and pepper for 15 min before grilling. Can use a regular tawa on medium heat — no grill needed. Add sunflower seeds or walnuts for healthy fat and crunch. Works as meal prep — grill a batch and assemble salads through the week.

recipes.description: Whole wheat pasta tossed in a spicy homemade tomato sauce — crushed tomatoes, garlic, dried chili flakes, olive oil, and fresh basil. A classic Italian staple that is genuinely easy to make at home and far lighter than restaurant versions. Whole wheat pasta gives nearly double the fibre of regular pasta.
recipes.notes: Use 70–80g dry pasta per serving. Keep oil to 1 tbsp — the flavour comes from garlic and tomatoes, not oil. Available in major supermarkets in Kathmandu (Bhatbhateni, Salesways). Add a small handful of kidney beans or chickpeas to boost protein if desired.

recipes.name_en: Whole Wheat Veggie and Egg Wrap
recipes.name_ne: होल व्हीट तरकारी र अण्डा र्‍याप
recipes.description: A whole wheat tortilla or roti spread with hummus or light cream cheese, filled with two scrambled eggs, lettuce, sliced tomato, cucumber, and thinly sliced capsicum. Rolled tight and eaten fresh. Quick, balanced, and one of the most popular grab-and-go continental lunches at home.
recipes.notes: A large chapati works perfectly if whole wheat tortillas are hard to find. Scramble eggs with just a pinch of salt and pepper — no oil needed in a non-stick pan. Add a squeeze of lemon or a dash of hot sauce for flavour. Takes under 15 minutes from start to finish.

recipes.description: Plain rice simmered long and slow in water or light broth until creamy and thick — the Chinese equivalent of comfort porridge. Topped with fresh ginger strips, spring onion, a soft-boiled egg, and a drizzle of sesame oil. Simple, deeply satisfying, and incredibly easy on the digestive system. One of the most widely eaten Chinese breakfasts across Asia.
recipes.notes: Use a 1:8 rice-to-water ratio and simmer on low for 30–40 min, stirring occasionally. Pressure cooker cuts this to under 15 min. Brown rice works but takes longer. Add shredded chicken or tofu for a higher-protein version (~380 cal). Soy sauce is available at major supermarkets in Kathmandu. Sesame oil is sold at Asian grocery stores and larger supermarkets.

recipes.name_en: Chinese Tomato and Egg Stir-fry with Rice
recipes.name_ne: चिनियाँ टमाटर र अण्डा भुटुवा भात
recipes.description: Arguably the most popular home-cooked Chinese dish in the world — two eggs scrambled in a hot wok with ripe tomatoes, a splash of soy sauce, and a drizzle of sesame oil, finished with spring onion. Served over a small bowl of steamed rice. The combination is tangy, savoury, and ready in under 15 minutes. A common breakfast or light meal across China and the broader Asian diaspora.
recipes.notes: The trick: cook eggs first on high heat, remove, then cook tomatoes briefly, and add eggs back at the end so they stay soft and fluffy. A tiny pinch of sugar balances the tomato acidity — do not skip it. Use 1 tsp sesame oil maximum. Easy to make with leftover rice from the night before. Substitute tomatoes with any seasonal vegetable if needed.

recipes.name_en: Greek Yogurt Parfait with Granola and Banana
recipes.description: Thick strained dahi (or hung curd) layered with crunchy granola and sliced banana — finished with a drizzle of honey. Ready in 5 minutes, no cooking required. High in protein and genuinely filling. One of the most popular continental breakfasts worldwide, and easily made at home in Kathmandu.
recipes.notes: Strain regular dahi overnight in a muslin cloth for Greek-style thickness. Granola is available at Bhatbhateni and Salesways. Substitute banana with any seasonal fruit (apple, mango, papaya). Avoid honey for strict diabetics — use a few berries instead.

recipes.description: Whole wheat toast topped with mashed ripe avocado seasoned with lemon juice, salt, and chili flakes, finished with a poached or fried egg. The combination of healthy fat from avocado and protein from egg keeps you full for hours. Probably the most iconic continental breakfast of the last decade — and for good reason.
recipes.notes: Avocados are available at Bhatbhateni, Salesways, and Sato Mall in Kathmandu. Use a ripe avocado (yields to gentle pressure). A fried egg works perfectly if poaching feels tricky. Add a pinch of cumin for a South Asian twist.

recipes.description: Just three ingredients — a ripe banana, rolled oats, and eggs — blended into a batter and cooked as small pancakes. Naturally gluten-free, no flour needed, no added sugar. Crispy on the outside, soft inside. A favourite high-protein breakfast among fitness-conscious home cooks.
recipes.notes: Blend 60g oats, 1 ripe banana, and 2 eggs until smooth. Cook on a lightly oiled non-stick pan over medium-low heat — flip when bubbles form. Top with a drizzle of honey or fresh fruit instead of syrup. The riper the banana, the sweeter and easier to blend.

recipes.description: Soaked yellow moong dal blended into a smooth batter with ginger, green chili, onion, and coriander, then cooked into thin savoury crepes on a hot tawa. One of the most nutritionally efficient Indian breakfasts — high protein, high fibre, very low fat, naturally gluten-free, and easy to digest.
recipes.notes: Soak 80g moong dal for at least 2 hours (or overnight for easier blending). The batter should be pourable like dosa batter — add water gradually. Cook on a well-seasoned tawa with minimal oil. Serve with mint chutney or a small bowl of dahi. Can add grated carrot or spinach to the batter.

recipes.name_en: Paneer Bhurji with Whole Wheat Roti
recipes.description: Crumbled paneer scrambled with onion, tomato, capsicum, turmeric, and cumin — served hot with two whole wheat rotis. The Indian equivalent of scrambled eggs, paneer bhurji is fast, high-protein, and deeply satisfying. A popular breakfast and light dinner across North Indian and Nepali households.
recipes.notes: Use 70–80g paneer per serving to keep calories in check. Crumble fine for best texture. A teaspoon of oil is enough — paneer releases some fat as it cooks. Add a pinch of garam masala at the end for aroma. Pairs well with a small bowl of dahi on the side.

recipes.description: Flattened rice (chiura/poha) tempered with mustard seeds, curry leaves, and turmeric, then tossed with sautéed onion, peas, and carrot — finished with roasted peanuts, fresh coriander, and a squeeze of lemon. Light, fragrant, and ready in 15 minutes. One of the most popular breakfasts across India and common in Nepali-Indian households.
recipes.notes: Rinse the poha briefly and drain — it should be moist but not soggy. The peanuts are essential for crunch and protein — do not skip them. Add a sliced potato for a more filling version. Serve immediately — poha becomes sticky and loses texture if left to sit.

recipes.name_en: Tuna Salad Sandwich on Whole Wheat
recipes.description: Canned tuna mixed with a spoonful of light mayo or hung curd, diced red onion, and a squeeze of lemon — layered with lettuce, sliced tomato, and cucumber between two slices of whole wheat bread. One of the simplest high-protein lunches you can make in under 10 minutes.
recipes.notes: Canned tuna (Del Monte, Ayam) is available at Bhatbhateni, Salesways, and larger general stores. Drain well before mixing. Hung curd works as a healthier mayo substitute. Toast the bread lightly for better texture. Add a pinch of black pepper and dijon mustard if available.

recipes.description: Cooked chickpeas tossed with diced tomato, cucumber, red onion, capsicum, and olives — dressed simply with olive oil, lemon juice, and dried oregano. Protein-rich, high in fibre, and genuinely refreshing. Works as a standalone lunch or alongside a bowl of soup.
recipes.notes: Canned chickpeas save time — drain and rinse well. Olives are available at larger supermarkets; skip or use sliced capsicum instead if unavailable. A small amount of crumbled paneer or feta adds protein if desired. Dress just before serving to keep vegetables crisp.

recipes.name_en: Chinese Stir-fried Chicken and Vegetables with Rice
recipes.name_ne: चिनियाँ भुटेको चिकन तरकारी र भात
recipes.description: Sliced chicken breast stir-fried on high heat with broccoli, carrot, capsicum, mushroom, and spring onion — seasoned with garlic, ginger, soy sauce, and a drizzle of sesame oil. Served over steamed rice. The home version of a Chinese restaurant classic, made with a fraction of the oil.
recipes.notes: High heat is the secret — use the hottest flame your stove allows and keep ingredients moving. Slice chicken thin for fast cooking. Cook each vegetable for 1–2 minutes max to keep the crunch. Substitute broccoli with bok choy, cabbage, or whatever vegetables are fresh.

recipes.description: Shredded chicken breast simmered in a clear ginger-garlic broth with egg noodles, bok choy, and mushrooms — finished with spring onion and a few drops of sesame oil. Comforting, high-protein, and genuinely low in fat. The kind of soup that works equally well as a light lunch or recovery meal.
recipes.notes: Simmer chicken in 2 cups water with ginger and garlic for 15 min, shred, then cook noodles in the same broth. Use low-sodium soy sauce to keep sodium reasonable. Rice noodles are a gluten-free option. Batch-cook the broth and store for up to 3 days.

recipes.description: Chickpeas slow-cooked in a deeply spiced tomato-onion gravy — cumin, coriander, amchur, garam masala, and dried chili — served with steamed white rice. A North Indian staple that is high in plant protein, fibre, and genuinely filling. One of the most popular vegan lunches in the region.
recipes.notes: Use dried chickpeas soaked overnight for best flavour, or canned for speed. The tamarind or amchur gives the signature tangy note — do not skip it. Batch-cook chole and refrigerate for up to 4 days. Serve with a small onion-tomato salad and a squeeze of lemon.

recipes.name_en: Tofu Buddha Bowl with Sesame Dressing
recipes.description: Pan-fried firm tofu over a base of brown rice or quinoa with shredded carrot, cucumber ribbons, red cabbage, and edamame (or cooked chickpeas), drizzled with a nutty sesame-soy-lemon dressing. Colourful, filling, and genuinely nutritious — a popular meal-prep lunch across fitness circles.
recipes.notes: Press tofu for 15 min before cooking to remove excess water — it crisps much better. Pan-fry in 1 tsp oil on medium-high until golden. Edamame is available frozen at larger Kathmandu supermarkets; chickpeas work just as well. Dressing: 1 tbsp tahini or peanut butter + 1 tsp soy + lemon juice + a splash of water to thin.

recipes.description: Whole black lentils and kidney beans slow-cooked until creamy with tomato, butter, cream, and aromatic spices — served with cumin-tempered white rice. A restaurant favourite that can be made at home with minimal active effort. Rich, deeply flavoured, and high in fibre. The key is long, slow cooking.
recipes.notes: Soak lentils overnight and pressure-cook for 20–25 min. Simmer the dal in the tomato-onion base for at least 20 more minutes — this is what develops the creaminess. Use just 1 tbsp butter and 1 tbsp cream for a lighter version. Batch-cooks well — flavour improves significantly the next day.

recipes.description: Chicken breast coated in a crust of garlic, dried herbs (oregano, thyme, rosemary), olive oil, and lemon zest, then cooked on a cast-iron tawa or baked until golden — served alongside roasted broccoli, carrot, capsicum, and zucchini. High protein, very low carb, excellent for fat loss or muscle gain clients who want a clean dinner.
recipes.notes: Marinate chicken in garlic, lemon juice, olive oil, and herbs for at least 30 min before cooking. A covered tawa on medium heat works perfectly — no oven required. Add a small serving of rice or roti to boost carbs for muscle-gain clients.

recipes.name_en: Whole Wheat Spaghetti with Chicken Mince Tomato Sauce
recipes.name_ne: होल व्हीट स्पागेटी र चिकन मिन्स
recipes.description: Whole wheat spaghetti tossed in a slow-cooked sauce of chicken mince, crushed tomatoes, garlic, onion, and Italian herbs. A leaner take on the classic Bolognese — chicken mince has a fraction of the saturated fat of beef while still delivering real depth of flavour. Popular and genuinely satisfying.
recipes.notes: Cook the mince until browned before adding tomatoes — this builds flavour. Simmer the sauce for at least 20 min on low heat. Whole wheat pasta is available at Bhatbhateni. Use 80g dry pasta per serving. Add a small sprinkle of parmesan at the end if available.

recipes.name_en: Palak Paneer with Whole Wheat Roti
recipes.description: Blanched spinach blended into a smooth green gravy with garlic, ginger, and mild spices, with cubes of paneer simmered through until soft. Served with two whole wheat rotis. One of the most popular Indian vegetarian dinners — iron-rich from the spinach, protein-rich from the paneer.
recipes.notes: Blanch spinach for 2 min then immediately cool in water — this keeps the green colour vivid. Use 70–80g paneer per serving. A tablespoon of cream is optional — the dish is excellent without it. Low-fat paneer significantly reduces the calorie count.

recipes.description: Fresh fish simmered in a light tomato-onion curry with mustard seeds, turmeric, coriander, and a small amount of coconut milk — served over steamed white rice. A universally beloved South Asian dinner, rich in omega-3 fatty acids and lean protein. Works with rohu, tilapia, bekti, or any firm white fish.
recipes.notes: Use firm white fish — it holds together better in curry. Marinate fish in turmeric and salt for 10 min before cooking. Add coconut milk at the end off the heat to prevent curdling. Fresh fish is available at Kalimati and local bazaars; frozen works fine too.

recipes.name_en: Mapo-Style Spiced Tofu with Steamed Rice
recipes.description: Soft tofu cubes simmered in a savoury, mildly spicy sauce of garlic, ginger, soy sauce, and chili — a simplified home-friendly version of the classic Sichuan mapo tofu, without the hard-to-find fermented bean paste. Quick, warming, and a very different way to eat tofu for clients who find it bland.
recipes.notes: Handle soft tofu gently — add it to the sauce last and simmer on low heat to warm through without breaking. For more protein, add 50g chicken mince or cooked edamame to the sauce. Sichuan peppercorns (if available) add the signature tingly numbing flavour.

recipes.name_en: Thai Green Curry with Chicken and Rice
recipes.description: Chicken breast simmered in a fragrant green curry sauce made from Thai green curry paste, light coconut milk, kaffir lime leaves, lemongrass, and Thai basil — with capsicum and zucchini. Served over steamed jasmine or white rice. A restaurant favourite that's surprisingly straightforward at home with the right paste.
recipes.notes: Thai green curry paste (Mae Ploy, Maesri) is available at larger Kathmandu supermarkets and Asian grocery stores. Use light coconut milk to keep calories reasonable. A full tin of regular coconut milk adds 200+ extra calories. Substitute chicken with tofu or mixed vegetables for a vegetarian version.

recipes.description: Store-bought or homemade hummus served with carrot sticks, cucumber batons, and capsicum strips. One of the most satisfying low-calorie snacks available — the chickpea base provides protein and fibre, the raw vegetables add crunch and micronutrients. Far better than biscuits or namkeen for the same hunger cue.
recipes.notes: Hummus is available ready-made at major supermarkets in Kathmandu. To make at home: blend cooked chickpeas, tahini, lemon, garlic, olive oil, and salt — takes 5 minutes. Batch-prep veggie sticks for the week and store in water in the fridge. Pairs well as a mid-afternoon snack.

recipes.name_en: Hard Boiled Eggs with Cucumber and Whole Wheat Crackers
recipes.description: Two hard-boiled eggs with sliced cucumber and four whole wheat crackers. Simple, portable, high-protein, and genuinely filling. The kind of snack that takes under 15 minutes to prepare and keeps hunger at bay for hours without adding unnecessary calories.
recipes.notes: Boil eggs in advance and refrigerate for up to 5 days in the shell — the most convenient meal-prep snack available. Whole wheat crackers are available at most supermarkets. Add a pinch of chaat masala or paprika on the eggs for flavour. Swap crackers for a small roti if preferred.
